original_abstract: In this study, we developed an aryl amino-substituted graphitic carbon nitride (g-C₃N₄) catalyst featuring atomically dispersed Mn sites, designed for the direct synthesis of hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) from seawater. The catalyst demonstrates exceptional reactivity, producing up to 2230 μM of H₂O₂ in alkaline water and 1800 μM in seawater over a 7-hour period. Furthermore, the material exhibits high stability and ease of recovery, maintaining its performance over multiple cycles. Mechanistic investigations reveal that rather than following the conventional two-electron oxygen reduction pathway, H₂O₂ generation proceeds via a two-electron water oxidation reaction (WOR). This process involves both a direct route, where photoinduced holes (h⁺) oxidize H₂O in a single step, and an indirect route, where h⁺ oxidizes hydroxide ions (OH⁻) to form hydroxyl radicals (•OH) that subsequently dimerize into H₂O₂. We employed a comprehensive suite of analytical techniques—including electron paramagnetic resonance, X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XANES/EXAFS), high-resolution TEM, XPS, and solid-state NMR—alongside multiscale molecular modeling and reactive molecular dynamics to characterize the catalytic centers at the atomic level and elucidate the underlying reaction dynamics.
humanized_abstract: Here, we report for the first time an aryl amino-substituted graphitic carbon nitride (g-C₃N₄) catalyst with atomically dispersed Mn sites to achieve highly efficient and selective direct H₂O₂ synthesis from seawater. The catalyst exhibits remarkable catalytic activity toward H₂O₂ production with the concentration reaching up to 2230 μM in alkaline water and 1800 μM in seawater solution over a period of 7 hours while possessing good stability and recyclability. The mechanisms for the DSH of H₂O₂ are attributed to the two-electron water oxidation reaction (WOR) in contrast to the conventional two-electron oxygen reduction reaction. The photoinduced holes directly oxidize H₂O in a single-step and thus catalyze the production of H₂O₂ in a direct pathway. The production of catalysis in DSH of H₂O₂ was achieved through the achievement of a catalyst that provides catalyst with high efficiency in terms of catalytic performance along with excellent stability towards aqueous environments. Thus, our findings present an efficient route for H₂O₂ production by upscaling the DSH reaction toward aqueous media including seawater. To achieve these unprecedented performances, the catalytic centers in the as-synthesized catalyst were identified at the atomic level using a battery of analytical techniques including electron paramagnetic resonance, X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XANES/EXAFS), high-resolution TEM, XPS, and solid-state NMR. Additionally, multiscale molecular modeling and reactive molecular dynamics studies at different scales including atomic, pair, and micro-structures revealed reaction dynamics that further sheds light towards their high efficiency toward catalyzing the DSH of H₂O₂.
